en.extract:
The Vanuatu streaked fantail (Rhipidura spilodera) is a species of bird in the family Rhipiduridae which is endemic to the Melanesian island of Vanuatu. It was formerly considered to be subspecies of the streaked fantail (now the New Caledonian streaked fantail). Its natural habitat is subtropical or tropical moist lowland forests.


Description
The Vanuatu streaked fantail is a small passerine with a long, fanned tail. The adults have white underparts and dark, grayish-brown wings and backs. Their breasts and lower throats are densely patterned with dark triangular spots and blurry streaking. They have white stripes in front of and behind their eyes. The juveniles tend to look like slightly duller versions of the adults, and they do not exhibit sexual dimorphism.


Taxonomy
The Vanuatu streaked fantail was formally described in 1870 by the English zoologist George Gray from a specimen that had been obtained from the island of Vanuatu by the collector Julius Brenchley. Gray coined the binomial name Rhipidura spilodera. The specific epithet combines the Ancient Greek spilos meaning "spot" with dera meaning "neck". This species was formerly treated as a subspecies of the streaked fantail (now the New Caledonian streaked fantail), but was split due to differences in morphology and vocalization.


Habitat and distribution
The Vanuatu streaked fantail exclusively lives in forested areas on the islands of Vanuatu in the South Pacific Ocean. They tend to avoid open areas and prefer dense forest patches. Their aversion to open areas segregates them by habitat from the gray fantail, which occupies the forest edge and second growth areas. Their preferred elevation changes based on the forest patches on each island, with some living at up to 800m above sea level and others preferring to stay below 500m.
Streaked fantails exclude non-endemic species from the forests they occupy through preemptive competition. By occupying the space and using the resources, other species with similar niches cannot invade the same space.


Behavior


Vocalization
The Vanuatu streaked fantail has a high-pitched, whistling song. They begin vocalizing up to an hour before dawn and will often be the first to start singing.


Reproduction
Not much is known about their reproduction, but they are thought to use similar strategies to other fantails. The New Zealand fantail, for example, has cooperative parenting where both the male and female incubate the eggs and brood the nestlings.
They create cup-shaped nests out of plant material that are bound using spiderwebs. Their eggs are cream in color with brown speckling on the larger side. Their nesting season typically lasts from September to January.


Diet
Vanuatu streaked fantails are insectivorous. They primarily forage by sallying in the mid to lower canopy and, less commonly, the undergrowth. They form mixed-species feeding flocks with other insectivores that use the same feeding strategy.


Conservation status
The Vanuatu streaked fantail currently faces no threats, but their populations are in decline. It is one of the most common and widespread understory birds in the forests of Vanuatu. The estimated population in the Loru Protected Area as of 1997 was 882 individuals. The IUCN Red List classifies them as a species of least concern.


References


External links
Vocalization on Birds of the World

The '''Vanuatu streaked fantail''' ('''''Rhipidura spilodera''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Rhipiduridae]] which is endemic to the [[Melanesia]]n island of [[Vanuatu]]. It was formerly considered to be [[subspecies]] of the streaked fantail (now the [[New Caledonian streaked fantail]]). Its natural [[habitat]] is subtropical or tropical moist lowland [[forest]]s.

== Description ==
The Vanuatu streaked fantail is a small [[passerine]] with a long, fanned tail. The adults have white underparts and dark, grayish-brown wings and backs. Their breasts and lower throats are densely patterned with dark triangular spots and blurry streaking. They have white stripes in front of and behind their eyes.<ref name=":0" /> The juveniles tend to look like slightly duller versions of the adults, and they do not exhibit [[sexual dimorphism]].<ref name=":2" />

==Taxonomy==
The Vanuatu streaked fantail was [[Species description|formally described]] in 1870 by the English zoologist [[George Robert Gray|George Gray]] from a specimen that had been obtained from the island of [[Vanuatu]] by the collector [[Julius Brenchley]]. Gray coined the [[binomial name]] ''Rhipidura spilodera''.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Gray | first=George Robert | author-link=George Robert Gray | year=1870 | title=Descriptions of new species of birds from the Solomon and Banks's groups of islands | journal=Annals and Magazine of Natural History | series=4th series | volume=5 | issue=29 | pages=327–331 [330] | doi=10.1080/00222937008696167 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/16355533 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor1-last=Mayr | editor1-first=Ernst | editor1-link=Ernst Mayr | editor2-last=Cottrell | editor2-first=G. William | year=1986 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=11 | publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts | page=548 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14484249 }}</ref> The specific epithet combines the [[Ancient Greek]] ''spilos'' meaning "spot" with ''dera'' meaning "neck".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=362 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n362/mode/1up }}</ref> This species was formerly treated as a subspecies of the streaked fantail (now the [[New Caledonian streaked fantail]]), but was split due to differences in [[Morphology (biology)|morphology]] and [[Bird vocalization|vocalization]].<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela C. Rasmussen | date=December 2023 | title=Orioles, drongos, fantails | work=IOC World Bird List Version 14.1 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/orioles/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=15 January 2024}}</ref>

== Habitat and distribution ==
The Vanuatu streaked fantail exclusively lives in forested areas on the islands of [[Vanuatu]] in the South Pacific Ocean. They tend to avoid open areas and prefer dense forest patches. Their aversion to open areas segregates them by habitat from the [[Grey fantail|gray fantail]],<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Andersen |first1=Michael J. |last2=Fatdal |first2=Lilly |last3=Iii |first3=William M. Mauck |last4=Smith |first4=Brian Tilston |date=2017-11-10 |title=An ornithological survey of Vanuatu on the islands of Éfaté, Malakula, Gaua, and Vanua Lava |url=https://checklist.pensoft.net/article/22128/ |journal=Check List |language=en |volume=13 |issue=6 |pages=755–782 |doi=10.15560/13.6.755 |doi-access=free |issn=1809-127X}}</ref> which occupies the forest edge and second growth areas.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Diamond |first1=Jared M. |last2=Marshall |first2=Adrian G. |date=1977-04-01 |title=Niche Shifts in New Hebridean Birds |url=https://doi.org/10.1071/MU9770061 |journal=Emu - Austral Ornithology |volume=77 |issue=2 |pages=61–72 |doi=10.1071/MU9770061 |bibcode=1977EmuAO..77...61D |issn=0158-4197|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Their preferred elevation changes based on the forest patches on each island, with some living at up to 800m above sea level and others preferring to stay below 500m.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kratter |first1=Andrew W. |last2=Kirchman |first2=Jeremy J. |last3=Steadman |first3=David W. |date=September 2006 |title=Upland Bird Communities on Santo, Vanuatu, Southwest Pacific |url=http://www.bioone.org/doi/abs/10.1676/05-082.1 |journal=The Wilson Journal of Ornithology |language=en |volume=118 |issue=3 |pages=295–308 |doi=10.1676/05-082.1 |issn=1559-4491|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

Streaked fantails exclude non-endemic species from the forests they occupy through preemptive [[Competition (biology)|competition]].<ref name=":1" /> By occupying the space and using the resources, other species with similar niches cannot invade the same space.

== Behavior ==

=== Vocalization ===
The Vanuatu streaked fantail has a high-pitched, whistling song. They begin [[Bird vocalization|vocalizing]] up to an hour before dawn and will often be the first to start singing.<ref name=":0" />

=== Reproduction ===
Not much is known about their reproduction, but they are thought to use similar strategies to other fantails. The [[New Zealand fantail]], for example, has cooperative parenting where both the male and female incubate the eggs and brood the nestlings.<ref>{{Cite web |title=New Zealand fantail {{!}} Pīwakawaka {{!}} New Zealand Birds Online |url=https://www.nzbirdsonline.org.nz/species/new-zealand-fantail |access-date=2025-11-04 |website=www.nzbirdsonline.org.nz |language=en}}</ref>

They create cup-shaped nests out of plant material that are bound using spiderwebs.<ref name=":2">{{Cite journal |last1=Boles |first1=Walter E. |last2=Christie |first2=David |date=2023 |title=Vanuatu Streaked Fantail (Rhipidura spilodera), version 1.0 |url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/strfan2/cur/introduction |journal=Birds of the World |language=en |doi=10.2173/bow.strfan2.01 |issn=2771-3105|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Their eggs are cream in color with brown speckling on the larger side. Their nesting season typically lasts from September to January.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Duron |first1=Quiterie |last2=Bourguet |first2=Edouard |last3=De Meringo |first3=Hélène |last4=Millon |first4=Alexandre |last5=Vidal |first5=Eric |date=2017-12-01 |title=Invasive rats strengthen predation pressure on bird eggs in a South Pacific island rainforest |url=http://academic.oup.com/cz/article/63/6/583/3044204 |journal=Current Zoology |language=en |volume=63 |issue=6 |pages=583–590 |doi=10.1093/cz/zox009 |issn=1674-5507 |pmc=5804218 |pmid=29492018}}</ref>

=== Diet ===
Vanuatu streaked fantails are [[Insectivore|insectivorous]].<ref name=":2" /> They primarily forage by [[Hawking (birds)|sallying]] in the mid to lower canopy and, less commonly, the undergrowth.<ref name=":2" /> They form [[Mixed-species foraging flock|mixed-species feeding flocks]] with other insectivores that use the same feeding strategy.<ref name=":2" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Sridhar |first1=Hari |last2=Beauchamp |first2=Guy |last3=Shanker |first3=Kartik |date=2009-08-01 |title=Why do birds participate in mixed-species foraging flocks? A large-scale synthesis |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0003347209002176 |journal=Animal Behaviour |volume=78 |issue=2 |pages=337–347 |doi=10.1016/j.anbehav.2009.05.008 |bibcode=2009AnBeh..78..337S |issn=0003-3472|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

== Conservation status ==
The Vanuatu streaked fantail currently faces no threats,<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bowen |first=Jennifer |date=December 1997 |title=The status of the avifauna of Loru Protected Area, Santo, Vanuatu |url=https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/bird-conservation-international/article/status-of-the-avifauna-of-loru-protected-area-santo-vanuatu/6C584DF965A375AA37CA59CB91792F81 |journal=Bird Conservation International |language=en |volume=7 |issue=4 |pages=331–344 |doi=10.1017/S0959270900001660 |issn=1474-0001}}</ref> but their populations are in decline.<ref name=":3">{{Cite web |date=12 June 2024 |title=Vanuatu Streaked Fantail |url=https://www.iucnredlist.org/species/103710780/264379656 |access-date=4 Nov 2025 |website=IUCN Red List}}</ref> It is one of the most common and widespread understory birds in the forests of [[Vanuatu]].<ref name=":0" /> The estimated population in the [https://www.planvivo.org/loru Loru Protected Area] as of 1997 was 882 individuals. The IUCN Red List classifies them as a species of least concern.<ref name=":3" />
